title: From Maneuver to Attrition: The Reluctant Return of Positional Warfare

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Giorgi Revishvili Thread

body_html:
One of the most uncomfortable implications of Zaluzhnyi's thesis is the return of attrition as the dominant mode of warfare. Western military culture — particularly in the United States and United Kingdom — has long treated attrition as the failure mode of incompetent generalship. Maneuver was the hallmark of sophistication. But the battlefield in Ukraine suggests that attrition may be structurally imposed by technology, not chosen by commanders.

When operational-level maneuver is denied by persistent surveillance and responsive fires, wars become grinding contests of industrial capacity, personnel resilience, and logistical endurance. The side that can sustain losses, produce munitions, and rotate forces more effectively gains the advantage — not through brilliance of maneuver but through relentless pressure distributed across a wide front.

This mirrors historical precedents from World War I, where technological conditions — machine guns, barbed wire, artillery — similarly collapsed the offense-defense balance and produced years of positional warfare. The difference today is that the enabling technologies are digital and distributed rather than industrial and static, but the effect on operational tempo is strikingly similar.

For Western militaries accustomed to short, decisive campaigns enabled by technological overmatch, this shift is deeply uncomfortable. It implies that future wars may be long, costly, and unresolvable through the rapid application of superior force. It demands not just tactical adaptation but strategic patience and industrial preparedness that many Western nations have allowed to atrophy.

The return of attrition is not a regression to primitive warfare. It is, paradoxically, the product of the most advanced technologies — drones, AI-enabled sensors, precision munitions — creating a battlefield where decisive action at scale is simply too costly to attempt.
